text: To evaluate LP performance, the LP model is trained on known facts and then assessed on its ability to predict unknown facts, that is, facts not observed during training. A simple but flawed way to adapt this procedure to OKGs is to sample a set of evaluation triples from the OKG and use the remaining portion of the OKG for training. To understand why this is problematic, consider the test triple ("NBC-TV", "has office in", "New York") and assume that the triple ("NBC", "has headquarter in", "NYC") is also included in the OKG. The latter triple effectively leaks the test fact. If such facts are not removed from the training data, a successful model merely paraphrases known facts rather than performing reasoning, that is, it does not predict genuinely new facts. In addition, we also want to measure whether there are other trivial explanations for predicting an evaluation fact. For instance, how much can be inferred from simple popularity statistics, such as only the mention, e.g. ("NBC-TV", ?), or only the relation, e.g. ("has office in", ?). Such non-relational information likewise does not require reasoning over the graph.
source_text: To evaluate LP performance, the LP model is trained on known facts and evaluated to predict unknown facts, i.e., facts not seen during training. A simple but problematic way to transfer this approach to OKGs is to sample a set of evaluation triples from the OKG and to use the remaining part of the OKG for training. To see why this approach is problematic, consider the test triple ("NBC-TV", "has office in", "New York") and suppose that the triple ("NBC", "has headquarter in", "NYC") is also part of the OKG. The latter triple essentially leaks the test fact. If we do not remove such facts from the training data, a successful models only paraphrases known facts but does not perform reasoning, i.e., does not predict genuinely new facts. Furthermore, we also want to quantify if there are other trivial explanations for the prediction of an evaluation fact. For example, how much can be predicted with simple popularity statistics, i.e., only the mention, e.g. ("NBC-TV", ?), or only the relation, e.g. ("has office in", ?). Such non-relational information also does not require reasoning over the graph.

text: Recently, Tao et al. (2017) introduced a hybrid referenced-unreferenced metric called RUBER, in which the metric is trained without human responses by bootstrapping negative samples directly from the dataset. However, referenced metrics, including RUBER because it is partly referenced, are not practical for evaluating dialogue models in an online setting—when the model is matched against a human agent (model-human) or another model agent (model-model)—because no reference response is available. In such settings, models are typically evaluated directly by humans, a process that is costly and requires careful training of annotators (Li et al., 2019).
source_text: Recently, Tao et al. (2017) proposed a hybrid referenced-unreferenced metric named RUBER, where the metric is trained without requiring human responses by bootstrapping negative samples directly from the dataset. However, referenced metrics (including RUBER, as it is part referenced) are not feasible for evaluation of dialogue models in an online setting-when the model is pitched against a human agent (model-human) or a model agent (model-model)-due to lack of a reference response. In this setting, models are usually eval-uated directly by humans, which is costly and requires careful annotator training (Li et al., 2019) .

text: Although most commercial dialogue systems depend on hand-crafted, narrow skills (e.g., see Zhou et al. (2020); Ram et al. (2018)), usually centered on distinct task-oriented functions such as setting alarms, adding calendar entries, and the like, our interest lies in models that exhibit a range of qualities in open-domain dialogue. Moreover, we concentrate on skills that can be learned end to end, since end-to-end learning offers the prospect of improved generalization to unseen domains.
source_text: While most commercial dialogue systems rely on hand-coded narrow skills (e.g., see Zhou et al. (2020) ; Ram et al. (2018) ), typically focusing on separate task-oriented features such as alarm setting, calendar entries, etc., we are interested in models that display various qualities in opendomain dialogue. Further, we focus on skills that can be learned end-to-end, as end-to-end learning affords the promise of better generalization to unseen domains.

text: The lower portion of Table 2 presents the performance of UNMT with XLM, which performs cross-lingual pretraining on concatenated non-parallel corpora (Lample and Conneau, 2019), as well as the performance of our AT/Bi-view AT models with anchored cross-lingual pretraining, namely ACP. The results indicate that our proposed AT methods remain superior even when equipped with cross-lingual pretraining. UNMT benefits substantially from XLM, attaining state-of-the-art unsupervised MT performance that surpasses Unsupervised SMT (Artetxe et al., 2019) and Unsupervised NMT (Lample et al., 2018b) on both close and distant language pairs. Nevertheless, ACP+AT/Bi-view AT consistently outperforms XLM+UNMT. In particular, on distant language pairs, ACP+Bi-view AT yields gains of 2.7–9.4 BLEU over the strong XLM+UNMT baseline. This suggests that AT/Bi-view AT with ACP induces closer language spaces through anchored pretraining and anchored training. We discuss this advantage in the analyses of Section 4.6.
source_text: The bottom part of Table 2 reports performances of UNMT with XLM, which conducts the crosslingual pretraining on concatenated non-parallel corpora (Lample and Conneau, 2019) , and performances of our AT/Bi-view AT with the anchored cross-lingual pretraining, i.e., ACP. The results show that our proposed AT approaches are still superior when equipped with the cross-lingual pretraining. UNMT obtains great improvement when combined with XLM, achieving state-of-the-art unsupervised MT performance better than Unsupervised SMT (Artetxe et al., 2019) and Unsupervised NMT (Lample et al., 2018b) across close and distant language pairs. ACP+AT/Bi-view AT performs consistently superior to XLM+UNMT. Especially on distant language pairs, ACP+Bi-view AT gains 2.7-9.4 BLEU improvements over the strong XLM+UNMT. This indicates that AT/Bi-view AT with ACP builds closer language spaces via anchored pretraining and anchored training. We present such advantage in the analyses of Section 4.6.

text: There have also been research efforts on parallel sentence extraction that do not depend on document alignment. Munteanu and Marcu (2002) extract parallel phrases from comparable corpora by using bilingual tries and seed dictionaries. Azpeitia et al. (2018) compute the Jaccard similarity of lexical translation overlap. Leong et al. (2018) employ an autoencoder together with a maximum entropy classifier. Bouamor and Sajjad (2018) examine cosine similarity over averaged multilingual word embeddings. Guo et al. (2018) propose a dual encoder architecture to learn multilingual sentence embeddings directly with additional negative examples. Wieting et al. (2019) derive sentence embeddings from sub-word embeddings and train a simpler model to discriminate between positive and negative examples. Artetxe and Schwenk (2019) build on the work of Guo et al. (2018) and obtain state-of-the-art results by considering the margins of cosine similarities between pairs of nearest neighbours.
source_text: There are also research efforts on parallel sentence extraction without the reliance on document alignment. Munteanu and Marcu (2002) acquire parallel phrases from comparable corpora using bilingual tries and seed dictionaries. Azpeitia et al. (2018) computes Jaccard similarity of lexical translation overlap. Leong et al. (2018) use an autoencoder and a maximum entropy classifier. Bouamor and Sajjad (2018) consider cosine similarity between averaged multilingual word embeddings. Guo et al. (2018) design a dual encoder model to learn multilingual sentence em-beddings directly with added negative examples. Wieting et al. (2019) obtain sentence embeddings from sub-word embeddings and train a simpler model to distinguish positive and negative examples. Artetxe and Schwenk (2019) refine Guo et al. (2018) 's work and achieve state-of-the-art by looking at the margins of cosine similarities between pairs of nearest neighbours.

text: With respect to contextualized models, the most prominent non-English contribution has been mBERT, which is released in two forms: (i) a single multilingual model covering 100 different languages and trained on Wikipedia data, and (ii) a single multilingual model for both Simplified and Traditional Chinese. Four fully trained monolingual ELMo models have been distributed for Japanese, Portuguese, German, and Basque5. In addition, 44 monolingual ELMo models6 were released by the HIT-SCIR team (Che et al., 2018) for the CoNLL 2018 Shared Task (Zeman et al., 2018), although their training sets were limited to 20 million words. A German BERT (Chan et al., 2019) and a French BERT model, CamemBERT (Martin et al., 2019), have also been released. Overall, however, no particular effort to build a set of high-quality monolingual contextualized representations has yet been demonstrated, or at least not at a scale comparable to that achieved for fixed word embeddings.
source_text: Regarding contextualized models, the most notable non-English contribution has been that of the mBERT , which is distributed as (i) a single multilingual model for 100 different languages trained on Wikipedia data, and as (ii) a single multilingual model for both Simplified and Traditional Chinese. Four monolingual fully trained ELMo models have been distributed for Japanese, Portuguese, German and Basque 5 ; 44 monolingual ELMo models 6 where also released by the HIT-SCIR team (Che et al., 2018) during the CoNLL 2018 Shared Task (Zeman et al., 2018) , but their training sets where capped at 20 million words. A German BERT (Chan et al., 2019) as well as a French BERT model (called CamemBERT) (Martin et al., 2019) have also been released. In general no particular effort in creating a set of highquality monolingual contextualized representations has been shown yet, or at least not on a scale that is comparable with what was done for fixed word embeddings.

text: We employ layer dropout with p = 0.2 and target embedding dropout with p = 0.1 (Gal and Ghahramani, 2016). We also apply label smoothing with p = 0.1 (Szegedy et al., 2016) and constrain the target embedding norm to 1 (Nguyen and Chiang, 2018). During inference, we use a beam size of 15 and length normalization with exponent 1.5. The batch size is adjusted dynamically according to input sequence length, with an average batch size of 36. Optimization is performed with Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2015) using an initial learning rate of 0.0003, which is reduced by a factor of 0.5 when validation BLEU fails to improve for 10 epochs at first and thereafter for 5 epochs. We do not apply L2 weight decay or Gaussian noise, and we use a single model replica. In addition, we use input feeding (Luong et al., 2015) and omit utterances longer than 1500 frames during training to satisfy memory constraints.
source_text: We use layer dropout with p = 0.2 and target embedding dropout with p = 0.1 (Gal and Ghahramani, 2016) . We apply label smoothing with p = 0.1 (Szegedy et al., 2016) and fix the target embedding norm to 1 (Nguyen and Chiang, 2018). For inference, we use beam of size 15 and length normalization with exponent 1.5. We set the batch size dynamically depending on the input sequence length with average batch size was 36. We use Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2015) with initial learning rate 0.0003, decayed by 0.5 when validation BLEU did not improve for 10 epochs initially and subsequently 5 epochs. We do not use L2 weight decay or Gaussian noise, and use a single model replica. We use input feeding (Luong et al., 2015) , and exclude utterances longer than 1500 frames in training for memory.

text: The procedure used to derive the dataset from OPIEC was as follows. At the outset, the dataset comprised more than 340M non-distinct triples, 5 enriched with metadata including the source sentence, linguistic annotations, confidence scores indicating the correctness of the extractions, and Wikipedia links in the triple’s subject or object. Triples of the following kinds were not useful for our purposes and were removed: (i) those with a confidence score < 0.3, 6 (ii) those containing personal or possessive pronouns, wh-determiners, adverbs, or determiners in one of their arguments, (iii) those whose relation came from an implicit appositive clause extraction, which we found to be very noisy, and (iv) those with a mention or relation longer than 10 tokens. This reduced the data to 80M non-distinct triples. We then lowercased the remaining 60M distinct triples and built an entity-mentions map from all triples with an annotated entity. We collected token frequencies and constructed a mention-token vocabulary containing the 200K most frequent tokens, as well as a relation-token vocabulary containing the 50K most frequent tokens. This was done to ensure that each token appeared at least ≈ 50 times. Finally, we retained only the triples whose tokens were included in these vocabularies, yielding the final 30M distinct triples.
source_text: The process of deriving the dataset from OPIEC was as follows. Initially, the dataset contained over 340M non-distinct triples, 5 which are enriched with metadata such as source sentence, linguistic annotations, confidence scores about the correctness of the extractions and the Wikipedia links in the triple's subject or object. Triples of the following types are not useful for our purpose and are removed: (i) having a confidence score < 0.3, 6 (ii) having personal or possessive pronouns, whdeterminer, adverbs or determiners in one of their arguments, (iii) having a relation from an implicit appositive clause extraction, which we found to be very noisy, and (iv) having a mention or a relation that is longer than 10 tokens. This left 80M nondistinct triples. Next, we lowercased the remaining 60M distinct triples and collect an entity-mentions map from all triples that have an annotated entity. We collected token counts and created a mention token vocabulary with the top 200K most frequent tokens, and a relation token vocabulary with the top 50K most frequent tokens. This was done to ensure that each token is seen at least ≈ 50 times. Finally, we kept only the triples whose tokens were contained in these vocabularies, i.e., the final 30M distinct triples.

text: Following the setting of (Hu et al., 2017), we primarily consider short text generation, with outputs no longer than 15 tokens, since this task is more amenable to both automatic and human evaluation. We conduct experiments on Yelp (Shen et al., 2017) and News Titles (Fu et al., 2018). Yelp is a corpus of restaurant reviews. We adopt the preprocessed version used in (Shen et al., 2017), which includes two polarity sentiment labels. For News Titles, we select titles from the Business, Entertainment, and Health categories for our experiments. Both Yelp and News Titles consist of relatively short texts. We remove texts longer than 15 words and then retain the top 8,900 and 10,000 words as the vocabularies for Yelp and News Titles, respectively. The dataset statistics are reported in Table 1. We discard the labels in the original training and validation splits. The original training split is used as the unlabeled corpus, the validation split is used to choose the best unsupervised models, and the test split is treated as the labeled conditional text.
source_text: Following the setting of (Hu et al., 2017) , we mainly focus on short text generation (no longer than 15 tokens), which is easier for both automatic and human evaluations. We use Yelp (Shen et al., 2017) and News Titles (Fu et al., 2018) for experiments. Yelp is a collection of restaurant reviews. We use the pre-processed version used in (Shen et al., 2017) , where two polarity sentiment labels are provided. For News Titles, we choose the titles belong to Business, Entertainment and Health categories for our experiments. Both Yelp and News Titles are datasets with relatively short text. We filter out text longer than 15 words, then choose the top 8,900 and 10,000 words as the vocabulary for Yelp and News Titles, respectively. The statistics of the two datasets are listed in Table 1 . We discard the labels in the original training and validation splits. We use the original training split as the unlabeled corpus; the validation split to select the best unsupervised models, and the test split as the labeled conditional text.

text: Because both interpretations are equally semantically plausible in the absence of contextual support, one might expect humans to select between HIGH and LOW at chance level. However, it has been widely shown that English speakers more often construe the relative clause as modifying the lower nominal rather than the higher nominal, that is, they exhibit a LOW bias (Carreiras and Clifton Jr., 1993; Frazier and Clifton, 1996; Carreiras and Clifton, 1999; Fernández, 2003). In fact, LOW bias is typologically much less common than HIGH bias (Brysbaert and Mitchell, 1996). A prototypical example of a language with a HIGH attachment bias is Spanish (see Carreiras and Clifton Jr., 1993; Carreiras and Clifton, 1999; Fernández, 2003).
source_text: As both interpretations are equally semantically plausible when no supporting context is given, we might expect that humans choose between HIGH and LOW at chance. However, it has been widely established that English speakers tend to interpret the relative clause as modifying the lower nominal more often than the higher nominal (i.e. they have a LOW bias; 1 Carreiras and Clifton Jr, 1993; Frazier and Clifton, 1996; Carreiras and Clifton, 1999; Fernández, 2003) . LOW bias is actually typologically much rarer than HIGH bias (Brysbaert and Mitchell, 1996) . A proto-typical example of a language with HIGH attachment bias is Spanish (see Carreiras and Clifton Jr, 1993; Carreiras and Clifton, 1999; Fernández, 2003) .

The reviews with the highest F1 are chosen as noisy variants of y, yielding the noisy set X(d) (see Figure 1). In total, we generate 2 * N noisy versions of y, i.e., X = X(c) ∪ X(d), and construct our synthetic training data D = {(X, y)} by producing |D| pseudoreview-summary pairs. Both noising strategies are required to obtain aspect diversity across the input reviews. Segment noising yields reviews that may refer to aspects absent from the summary, whereas document noising produces reviews whose content is similar to the summary. Using only one of the noise functions reduces performance (see the ablation studies in Section 5). Examples of these noisy versions are provided in the Appendix.

text: To identify which explanations matter most, we organize them into several semantic categories (see Appendix A.3) and then add them cumulatively to the NoExp baseline. In particular, for Spouse we divide the explanations into the groups MARRIED (10 explanations), CHILDREN (5), ENGAGED (3), NEGA-TIVES (13), and MISC (9). Figure 3 shows that ExpBERT matches the performance of the NoExp baseline with 20x less data on Spouse (Left) and with 3x less data on TACRED (Right). We find that adding additional explanation groups improves performance (Table 3), which suggests that wide coverage of different explanatory factors may be beneficial for performance. We also observe that the MARRIED group, which contains paraphrases of {o 1 } is married to {o 2 }, by itself improves over NoExp, suggesting that multiple paraphrases of the same explanation can improve performance.
source_text: To understand which explanations are important, we group explanations into a few semantic categories (details in Appendix A.3) and cumulatively add them to the NoExp baseline. In particular, we break down explanations for Spouse into the Figure 3 : ExpBERT matches the performance of the NoExp baseline with 20x less data on Spouse (Left), and with 3x less data on TACRED (Right). groups MARRIED (10 explanations), CHILDREN (5 explanations), ENGAGED (3 explanations), NEGA-TIVES (13 explanations) and MISC (9 explanations). We find that adding new explanation groups helps performance (Table 3) , which suggests that a broad coverage of various explanatory factors could be helpful for performance. We also observe that the MARRIED group (which contains paraphrases of {o 1 } is married to {o 2 }) alone boosts performance over NoExp, which suggests that a variety of paraphrases of the same explanation can improve performance.

text: When narrating stories, people rely on both their own experiences (episodic knowledge; Conway et al., 1996, 2003) and their broader understanding of the world (semantic knowledge; Bartlett, 1932; Oatley, 1999). For instance, in Figure 1 (top), a remembered account of a birth will likely describe concrete events from that day, drawing heavily on the author’s episodic memory (Tulving, 1972). On the ….her husband called me and then drove her to the hospital. I joined her at the hospital. When we got the hospital, things became complicated. Her husband did everything he could to stay with her and keep her strong. She eventually delivered perfectly.
source_text: When telling stories, people draw from their own experiences (episodic knowledge; Conway et al., 1996 Conway et al., , 2003 and from their general world knowledge (semantic knowledge; Bartlett, 1932; Oatley, 1999) . For example, in Figure 1 (top), a recalled story about a birth will likely recount concrete events from that day, relying heavily on the author's episodic memory (Tulving, 1972) . On the ….her husband called me and then drove her to the hospital. I joined her at the hospital. When we got the hospital things got complicated. Her husband tried his best to be with her and to keep her strong. She eventually delivered perfectly.

First, we identify subphrase pairs in the input phrase that conform to parse-tree boundaries, with each pair comprising nonoverlapping phrases (Step 1). Because the objective is to learn general syntax-driven reorderings, we abstract away the two subphrases and substitute nonterminal symbols for them, preserving only the constituent tag information. For instance, Figure 3 presents three phrase pairs that can be abstracted to produce the reduced sentence forms. We then apply a seq2seq model to generate rearrangements for each abstracted phrase (Step 2). Finally, this higher-level rearrangement is combined with recursively constructed phrase rearrangements inside the abstracted phrases to produce sentence-level rearrangements (Step 3).

First, we select sub-phrase pairs of the input phrase that respect parse-tree boundaries, where each pair consists of non-overlapping phrases (Step 1). Since the aim is to learn generic syntax-governed rearrangements, we abstract out the two sub-phrases, and replace them with non-terminal symbols, retaining only the constituent tag information. For example, we show three phrase pairs in Figure 3 that can be abstracted away to yield the reduced forms of the sentences. We then use a seq2seq model to obtain rearrangements for each abstracted phrase (Step 2). Finally, this top-level rearrangement is combined with recursively-constructed phrase rearrangements within the abstracted phrases to obtain sentence-level rearrangements (Step 3).

text: Natural language generation (NLG) from structured data or knowledge (Gatt and Krahmer, 2018) is an important research problem for a variety of NLP applications. Examples include task-oriented dialogue and question answering (He et al., 2017; Ghazvininejad et al., 2018; Su et al., 2016; Saha et al., 2018; Yin et al., 2016), as well as interdisciplinary domains such as medicine (Hasan and Farri, 2019; Cawsey et al., 1997) and healthcare (Hasan and Farri, 2019; DiMarco et al., 2007). There is substantial promise in deploying automatic NLG systems across many real-world applications. In recent years, deep neural network-based NLG systems have been developed, including those in the E2E challenge (Novikova et al., 2017), WEATHER-GOV (Liang et al., 2009), and more complex settings such as WIKIBIO (Liu et al., 2018) and RO-TOWIRE (Wiseman et al., 2017). Relative to conventional slot-filling pipeline methods, such neural approaches considerably reduce the need for feature engineering and enhance both text diversity and fluency.
source_text: Natural language generation (NLG) from structured data or knowledge (Gatt and Krahmer, 2018) is an important research problem for various NLP applications. Some examples are taskoriented dialog, question answering (He et al., 2017; Ghazvininejad et al., 2018; Su et al., 2016; Saha et al., 2018; Yin et al., 2016) and interdisciplinary applications such as medicine (Hasan and Farri, 2019; Cawsey et al., 1997) and healthcare (Hasan and Farri, 2019; DiMarco et al., 2007) . There is great potential to use automatic NLG systems in a wide range of real-life applications. Recently, deep neural network based NLG systems have been developed, such as those seen in the E2E challenge (Novikova et al., 2017) , WEATHER-GOV (Liang et al., 2009) , as well as more complex ones such as WIKIBIO (Liu et al., 2018) and RO-TOWIRE (Wiseman et al., 2017) . Compared to traditional slot-filling pipeline approaches, such neural-based systems greatly reduce feature engineering efforts and improve text diversity as well as fluency.

text: We note that semi-structured premises compelled annotators to invoke world knowledge and common sense (KCS); 48.75% of instances in the Dev set require KCS. By comparison, in the MultiNLI corpus, KCS is required for 25.72% of examples. We suspect that this is because information about the entities and their types is not explicitly provided in tables and must therefore be inferred. In making these inferences, our annotators drew on their knowledge of the world, including facts about weather, seasons, and widely recognized social and cultural norms and facts. One illustration of such common sense is the hypothesis that “X was born in summer” for a person whose date of birth is in May in New York. We expect that the INFOTABS data can serve as a foundation for investigating common-sense reasoning, alongside other recent work such as that of Talmor et al. (2019),Neutral hypotheses are more likely to be subjective or out-of-table because nearly anything subjective, or anything not mentioned in the table, is a neutral statement. Even so, we found that in all evaluations in Appendix E, except those involving the adversarial α 2 test set, our models found neutrals almost as difficult as the other two labels, with only an ≈ 3% gap between the F-scores of the neutral label and the next best label.
source_text: We observe that semi-structured premises forced annotators to call upon world knowledge and common sense (KCS); 48.75% instances in the Dev set require KCS. (In comparison, in the MultiNLI data, KCS is needed in 25.72% of examples.) We conjecture that this is because information about the entities and their types is not explicitly stated in tables, and have to be inferred. To do so, our annotators relied on their knowledge about the world including information about weather, seasons, and widely known social and cultural norms and facts. An example of such common sense is the hypothesis that "X was born in summer" for a person whose date of birth is in May in New York. We expect that the INFOTABS data can serve as a basis for studying common sense reasoning alongside other recent work such as that of Talmor et al. (2019),Neutral hypotheses are more inclined to being subjective/out-of-table because almost anything subjective or not mentioned in the table is a neutral statement. Despite this, we found that in all evaluations in Appendix E (except those involving the adversarial α 2 test set), our models found neutrals almost as hard as the other two labels, with only an ≈ 3% gap between the F-scores of the neutral label and the next best label.
